meta.title: DeepSeek-V3.2: Pushing the Frontier of Open Large Language Models

source.title: DeepSeek-V3.2: Pushing the Frontier of Open Large Language Models

slides.title: DeepSeek-V3.2: Pushing the Frontier of Open Large Language Models
slides.speaker_notes: One sentence: DeepSeek made long-context attention sparse, held everything else fixed, and landed an open-weights model in the GPT-5 band at a fraction of the long-context cost.

slides.title: One architectural change: make attention sparse
slides.speaker_notes: The whole paper is one deliberate change over V3.1-Terminus.

slides.action_title: DSA: a ==k|lightning indexer plus top-k selection==, bolted onto MLA

figure.caption: Fig. 2 | Attention architecture of DeepSeek-V3.2 — DSA instantiated under MLA (green = the sparse path).

slides.annotation: A lightweight “lightning indexer” scores past tokens; a Top-k Selector keeps only the highest-scoring key-value entries, so core attention runs over a sparse subset instead of the full sequence.
slides.speaker_notes: The green path is the change: a cheap indexer scores every past token, a top-k selector keeps the best 2048, and the expensive core attention only sees that subset.

slides.action_title: Core attention drops from ==k|quadratic to linear-in-k==
slides.note: L = sequence length; k = number of selected key-value tokens per query (V3.2 uses ==k = 2048==).
slides.speaker_notes: Fixed k, so the quadratic term becomes effectively linear in context length.

slides.action_title: Long-context inference gets ==k|materially cheaper== on real hardware

figure.caption: Fig. 3 | Inference cost of DeepSeek-V3.1-Terminus vs DeepSeek-V3.2 on H800 GPUs.

slides.annotation: Measured on the deployed service (H800, $2 / GPU-hour): V3.2's cost stays far flatter as context grows, while the dense predecessor climbs steeply.
slides.speaker_notes: Not just complexity on paper — measured dollars on the deployed service. The dense curve climbs; the sparse one stays flat.

slides.title: Does the cheap model still reason?
slides.speaker_notes: The obvious worry: sparsity costs capability. The scoreboard says no.

slides.action_title: V3.2-Thinking sits with GPT-5 and Gemini; Speciale ==p|tops the chart==

figure.caption: Fig. 1 | Benchmark of DeepSeek-V3.2 and counterparts across reasoning and agentic tasks.

slides.annotation: Across reasoning (AIME, HMMT, HLE, Codeforces) and agentic (SWE, Terminal-Bench, τ²-Bench) tasks, the shipped V3.2-Thinking is ==k|competitive with== GPT-5-High and Gemini-3.0-Pro; the high-compute Speciale variant is ==p|often highest== and reaches ==p|olympiad gold== (IMO, IOI, ICPC WF, CMO 2025).
slides.speaker_notes: Shipped model: same band as the closed frontier. High-compute Speciale: frequently on top, and gold at four 2025 olympiads — at an RL budget exceeding 10% of pre-training.

slides.action_title: An open model within striking distance, ==p|winning some==

table.caption: Selected benchmarks — DeepSeek-V3.2-Thinking vs leading closed models (row-best in bold)
table.footnote: Closed-model figures are vendor-reported. — = not reported.

slides.speaker_notes: Knowledge and math: a few points behind Gemini. Software engineering: it wins SWE Multilingual outright at 70.2 and beats GPT-5 and Claude on Terminal-Bench. Caveat: closed numbers are vendor-reported.

slides.title: Open weights, frontier band, linear-cost long context — read the paper
slides.speaker_notes: If you self-host long-context workloads, the cost curve alone is the reason to look. arXiv 2512.02556.

slides.title: References
entries: DeepSeek-AI (2025). DeepSeek-V3.2: Pushing the Frontier of Open Large Language Models. arXiv:2512.02556. https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2512.02556
